items.prompt: Which ancient Greek philosopher, who never wrote anything down, was sentenced to death by drinking hemlock?
items.explanation:
Athenian philosopher (c. 470–399 BCE); foundational figure of Western philosophy
Never wrote anything — known entirely through the writings of his students, especially Plato
The 'Socratic method' — teaching through relentless questioning — is still used in law schools
Convicted of 'corrupting the youth' and 'impiety' by an Athenian jury of 501; sentenced to death
Refused to escape prison, arguing a citizen must obey the law even when unjust
Drank hemlock calmly, as described in Plato's *Phaedo* — one of history's most famous deaths
items.cardFront: Socratic method, never wrote anything, death by hemlock

items.prompt: Which philosopher wrote *The Republic* and founded the Academy in Athens?
items.explanation:
Athenian philosopher (c. 428–348 BCE); student of Socrates, teacher of Aristotle
Founded the Academy in Athens (c. 387 BCE) — often called the first university in the Western world
*The Republic* introduces the Allegory of the Cave, Theory of Forms, and the philosopher-king concept
Wrote in dialogues with Socrates as the main character — hard to separate Plato's views from Socrates's
Alfred North Whitehead said all Western philosophy is 'a series of footnotes to Plato'
'Plato' was likely a nickname meaning 'broad' — his real name may have been Aristocles
items.cardFront: *The Republic*, Academy in Athens, Theory of Forms

items.prompt: Which philosopher tutored Alexander the Great and wrote foundational works on logic, ethics, politics, and biology?
items.explanation:
Greek philosopher (384–322 BCE); student of Plato, tutor to Alexander the Great
Founded the Lyceum in Athens; his followers were called Peripatetics ('those who walk about')
Invented formal logic (the syllogism) and classified knowledge into disciplines still used today
*Nicomachean Ethics* defines virtue as the 'golden mean' between extremes
Wrote on biology, physics, metaphysics, poetry, theater, music, rhetoric, and politics
His works dominated European intellectual life for nearly 2,000 years until the Scientific Revolution
items.cardFront: Tutored Alexander the Great, formal logic, the Lyceum

items.prompt: Which philosopher declared 'I think, therefore I am' and is called the father of modern philosophy?
items.explanation:
French philosopher and mathematician (1596–1650)
'Cogito, ergo sum' ('I think, therefore I am') — the one thing that survives radical doubt
Invented the Cartesian coordinate system, bridging algebra and geometry
*Meditations on First Philosophy* (1641) is a cornerstone of epistemology
Mind-body dualism ('Cartesian dualism') separates mental substance from physical — still debated today
Died in Stockholm in 1650, possibly from pneumonia caught giving 5 AM philosophy lessons to Queen Christina of Sweden
items.cardFront: 'Cogito, ergo sum,' Cartesian coordinates, mind-body dualism

items.prompt: Which German philosopher wrote *Critique of Pure Reason* and formulated the categorical imperative?
items.explanation:
German philosopher (1724–1804); central figure of the Enlightenment
*Critique of Pure Reason* (1781) argues knowledge requires both sensory experience and mental categories
The categorical imperative: act only according to rules you could will to be universal laws
Never traveled more than 10 miles from Königsberg (now Kaliningrad, Russia) his entire life
Neighbors set their clocks by his daily walk — he was that punctual, except once (reading Rousseau's *Emile*)
Synthesized rationalism (Descartes, Leibniz) and empiricism (Locke, Hume) into a new framework

items.prompt: Which philosopher proclaimed 'God is dead' and introduced the concept of the Übermensch?
items.explanation:
German philosopher (1844–1900); one of the most provocative and misappropriated thinkers in history
'God is dead' (from *The Gay Science*, 1882) — not a celebration but a warning about the collapse of moral foundations
The Übermensch ('overman/superman') concept from *Thus Spoke Zarathustra* (1883–1885)
Nazi appropriation of his work was largely driven by his sister Elisabeth, who edited his writings after his collapse
Suffered a mental breakdown in 1889 (possibly from syphilis or brain cancer); spent his last 11 years incapacitated
Also introduced 'will to power,' 'eternal recurrence,' and 'master-slave morality'

items.prompt: Which philosopher co-wrote *The Communist Manifesto* and authored *Das Kapital*?
items.explanation:
German philosopher, economist, and revolutionary (1818–1883)
*The Communist Manifesto* (1848, with Friedrich Engels): 'Workers of the world, unite!'
*Das Kapital* (Vol. 1, 1867) — his magnum opus analyzing capitalism's internal contradictions
Historical materialism: economic forces, not ideas, drive historical change through class struggle
Lived most of his adult life in poverty in London; several of his children died young
Buried in Highgate Cemetery, London; his grave monument reads 'Workers of all lands unite'

items.prompt: Which Scottish philosopher argued that all knowledge comes from sense experience and that causation is merely habitual association?
items.explanation:
Scottish philosopher (1711–1776); the foremost empiricist of the Enlightenment
*A Treatise of Human Nature* (1739–1740) — his masterwork, which he said 'fell dead-born from the press'
The 'is-ought problem' (Hume's guillotine): you cannot derive moral 'oughts' from factual 'is' statements
Argued causation is not observable — we only see constant conjunction, not necessary connection
Kant credited Hume with 'awakening him from his dogmatic slumber'
Also a historian; his six-volume *History of England* was a bestseller for decades
items.cardFront: Empiricism, is-ought problem, 'awakened Kant from dogmatic slumber'

items.prompt: Which philosopher proposed that the mind begins as a 'blank slate' and articulated natural rights to life, liberty, and property?
items.explanation:
English philosopher (1632–1704); 'Father of Liberalism'
*An Essay Concerning Human Understanding* (1689): the mind at birth is a tabula rasa (blank slate)
*Two Treatises of Government* (1689): government derives authority from the consent of the governed
Articulated natural rights to 'life, liberty, and property' — Jefferson adapted this for the Declaration of Independence
Influenced the U.S. Constitution, the French Revolution, and classical liberal political theory
Trained as a physician; served as personal doctor and advisor to Lord Shaftesbury
items.cardFront: Tabula rasa, natural rights, *Two Treatises of Government*

items.prompt: Which philosopher described life without government as 'nasty, brutish, and short' in *Leviathan*?
items.explanation:
English philosopher (1588–1679); foundational social contract theorist
*Leviathan* (1651): without a strong sovereign, life is 'solitary, poor, nasty, brutish, and short'
The 'state of nature' is a war of 'every man against every man' — people surrender freedom for security
Advocated absolute sovereignty, not democracy — the sovereign's power must be undivided
Lived to 91 despite the era; reportedly said his last words were 'I am about to take my last voyage'
Tutored Charles II in mathematics during the English Civil War exile in Paris

items.prompt: Which philosopher, excommunicated from the Amsterdam Jewish community, argued that God and Nature are the same substance?
items.explanation:
Dutch philosopher (1632–1677); born into the Portuguese Jewish community in Amsterdam
Excommunicated (cherem) from the synagogue at age 23 for 'abominable heresies'
*Ethics* (1677, published posthumously) — constructed in geometric proofs like Euclid
God and Nature are identical ('Deus sive Natura') — pantheism that scandalized both Jews and Christians
Made his living grinding optical lenses; inhaling glass dust likely contributed to his death at 44
Einstein, when asked if he believed in God, replied: 'I believe in Spinoza's God'

items.prompt: Which philosopher wrote the *Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus* and later the *Philosophical Investigations*, revolutionizing philosophy of language twice?
items.explanation:
Austrian-British philosopher (1889–1951); transformed philosophy of language in two opposing phases
*Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus* (1921) — the only book published in his lifetime; final line: 'Whereof one cannot speak, thereof one must be silent'
*Philosophical Investigations* (published posthumously, 1953) repudiated much of the *Tractatus*
Born into one of Europe's wealthiest families (Viennese steel); gave away his entire inheritance
Served in WWI on the Austrian side; finished the *Tractatus* in an Italian prisoner-of-war camp
Three of his four brothers died by suicide; he struggled with suicidal thoughts himself throughout his life

items.prompt: Which philosopher wrote *Being and Nothingness* and declared 'existence precedes essence'?
items.explanation:
French philosopher, novelist, and playwright (1905–1980); leading existentialist
'Existence precedes essence' — humans have no predetermined nature; we define ourselves through choices
*Being and Nothingness* (1943) — his major philosophical work, written during the German occupation of Paris
Declined the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1964 — the only person to voluntarily refuse it
Lifelong partner of Simone de Beauvoir; they had an open relationship that scandalized postwar France
Also wrote the play *No Exit* (1944), source of the famous line 'Hell is other people'

items.prompt: Which philosopher wrote *The Second Sex* and is considered a founding figure of modern feminism?
items.explanation:
French philosopher and writer (1908–1986); existentialist and feminist theorist
*The Second Sex* (1949): 'One is not born, but rather becomes, a woman' — foundational text of second-wave feminism
Won the Prix Goncourt for her novel *The Mandarins* (1954)
Lifelong partner of Jean-Paul Sartre; both maintained additional relationships openly
The Ethics of Ambiguity (1947) is her major contribution to existentialist ethics
Buried alongside Sartre in the Montparnasse Cemetery in Paris
items.cardFront: *The Second Sex*, 'One is not born, but rather becomes, a woman'

items.prompt: Which ancient Chinese philosopher's teachings on ethics, family, and governance became the foundation of East Asian moral philosophy for over two millennia?
items.explanation:
Chinese philosopher (551–479 BCE); Latinized name from Kong Fuzi ('Master Kong')
The *Analects* (compiled by disciples) contain his teachings on virtue, propriety, and filial piety
Emphasized ren (benevolence), li (ritual propriety), and xiao (filial piety) as foundations of a moral society
Confucianism became China's state philosophy under Emperor Wu of Han (141–87 BCE)
Held minor government posts in the state of Lu but spent years wandering, seeking a ruler who'd adopt his ideas
The Confucius Temple in Qufu, Shandong Province, has been a site of veneration for over 2,000 years
items.cardFront: *Analects*, filial piety, foundation of East Asian ethics
